title: Mobile malware survey (Felt et al.) — methodology, data set, novelty/amusement, selling user info
html:
TL;DR

A page from Adrienne Felt et al.'s 2011 uc berkeley mobile-malware survey — the methodology + data set + first two incentive categories. 46 distinct pieces of mobile malware from Jan 2009 to June 2011, harvested from anti-virus vendor reports (Symantec, F-Secure, Fortiguard, Lookout, Panda). 4 iOS, 24 Symbian, 18 Android.

The most-quoted finding lives here: all 4 iOS malware samples appeared within a single month in 2009, while Android went from zero to ~all of its sample count in 2011 alone. The paper introduces the incentive taxonomy: novelty/amusement, exfiltration, premium-rate calls/SMS, ad spam, credential theft, SEO, ransom.

Why it's in the graph

Pairs naturally with the 2025 04 27 screenshot 2025 04 27 at 7 40 21 pm e4e5aad8 biometrics screenshots — Yahir's spring-summer 2025 osu coursework was clearly security-shaped. (Likely CS 419 or a similar mobile/systems-security course.) This is the descriptive-empirical companion to the more theoretical biometrics survey.

The IMEI laundering and inter-app-data-theft sections also seed a real entrepreneurial angle: every defense the paper proposes ("alternate globally-unique device ID", "stricter inter-app permissions") is a feature that did or didn't end up shipping in iOS/Android over the next decade. Worth re-reading with 2026 eyes.

What's actually on the page

  • Methodology — anti-virus DB combing + dual-confirmation rule + variant coalescence. Personal spyware and PoCs deliberately excluded.
  • The 46-sample dataset — published at cs.berkeley.edu/~daw/malware.html (David Wagner's group).
  • ios vs symbian vs android os split — 4/24/18. Symbian dominates because it was still installed everywhere and symbian's permission model is strictly weaker than iOS's (Symbian Signed apps get all privileges automatically).
  • Incentive #1 — novelty/amusement — Ikee.A changed iPhone wallpapers; Smspacem texted anti-religion messages from Android. 6/46 samples. Predicted to fade as profit motives mature.
  • Incentive #2 — exfiltrating user info — 28/46 samples. APIs leak location, contacts, browser history, installed apps, IMEI. Hypothesis: sold downstream for behavioral profiling.
  • imei laundering — stolen-phone IMEIs get blacklisted; thieves swap in fresh ones harvested by malware. Defense: app-facing pseudo-IMEI.

Connection lines worth pulling

  • The incentive taxonomy is a template. Apply the same frame to any new vector — LLM jailbreaks, MCP-server malware, browser-extension malware. Where's the money? is the right first question.
  • The 6/46 amusement → 0/all profit trajectory is the same arc desktop malware traveled. Mobile is a re-run on faster tape. Worth keeping near disruption as an example of "new platforms compress old transitions."
  • The fact that all iOS malware appeared in one month is itself an argument for permission-model design as a security primitive — comparable to how ios's mandatory user-confirmation for SMS killed the entire premium-SMS-fraud vector on Apple devices (see 2025 06 01 mobile malware credentials sms).

Wikilinks

uc berkeley · mobile malware · ios · android os · symbian · imei laundering · osu · premium rate sms fraud · two factor authentication bypass · surveillance state · 2025 06 01 mobile malware credentials sms · 2025 04 27 screenshot 2025 04 27 at 7 40 21 pm e4e5aad8

Filename note: the second mobile-malware page is summarized at 2025 06 01 mobile malware credentials sms — same-day screenshot, continuation of this paper.
